qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.title: Default-disabled authentication exposes the admin API and permits unauthenticated plugin-based code execution
qualifying_vulnerability_assessments.advisory_match_reason: The finding relies on `secureEnabled` being off and exploits unauthenticated HTTP admin routes (`/api/users`, `/api/upload`, `/api/plugins`) to reach plugin loading, while the advisory fixes missing authorization for WebSocket device-tag writes and device enable toggles in `server/runtime/index.js` even when security is enabled.

target_advisory_judgement.reason: No final qualifying finding identifies the advisory's unauthenticated WebSocket write authorization flaw in `server/runtime/index.js`; the only real qualifying finding is a separate default-auth-disabled HTTP admin API exposure that uses upload and plugin endpoints for RCE.
evidence: The advisory summary states the issue is unauthenticated remote device tag modification via WebSockets and explicitly says it affects deployments even with `runtime.settings.secureEnabled=true`.

The advisory patch `eb2d8a20964ce7acaa0f442a181390a5f726a1ae` changes `server/runtime/index.js` to track socket authentication state and adds `isSocketWriteAuthorized()` guards before `DEVICE_VALUES` `set` operations and `DEVICE_ENABLE` requests.

The session's only final qualifying finding, `default_auth_admin_api_rce`, instead cites `server/settings.default.js`, `server/api/apikeys/verify-api-or-token.js`, `server/api/index.js`, and the HTTP routes `/api/users`, `/api/upload`, and `/api/plugins`, with an exploit path that depends on `secureEnabled` being off by default.
